hypotheses: Relaxation during the weekend is positively associated with joviality, serenity, and self-assurance (Hypotheses 1a–1c) and negatively associated with hostility, fear, sadness, and fatigue (Hypotheses 1d–1g).
Mastery experiences during the weekend are positively associated with joviality (Hypothesis 2a), serenity (Hypothesis 2b), and self-assurance (Hypothesis 2c).
Control during the weekend is positively associated with joviality (Hypothesis 3a), serenity (Hypothesis 3b), and self-assurance (Hypothesis 3c).
Psychological detachment during the weekend is positively associated with joviality, serenity, and self-assurance and negatively associated with sadness, hostility, fear, and fatigue.
Non-work hassles during the weekend are positively associated with hostility, fear, sadness, and fatigue.
claim.statement: Weekend recovery experiences significantly predict employees’ affective states at the end of the weekend and during the subsequent workweek.

data.source: Self-report surveys (Recovery Experience Questionnaire and PANAS-X)
data.wave_or_subset: Three measurement points: Time 1 (Friday after work), Time 2 (Sunday evening), Time 3 (following Friday)
data.notes: Data are self-reported; potential common-method bias noted.

method.description: The study employed a three-wave longitudinal design in which 229 preschool teachers completed surveys measuring their recovery experiences over the weekend and their affective states before, immediately after, and one week after the weekend. Confirmatory factor analyses validated measurement models, and hierarchical regression analyses tested the impact of weekend experiences on subsequent affect while controlling for baseline affect and demographics.
steps: Recruit 229 preschool teachers and obtain informed consent.
Administer Time 1 survey on Friday after work measuring baseline affect and demographics.
Administer Time 2 survey on Sunday evening measuring weekend recovery experiences (relaxation, mastery, control, detachment, non-work hassles) and affective states.
Administer Time 3 survey on the following Friday measuring affective states during the workweek.
Conduct confirmatory factor analyses for recovery experiences and affect measures.
Run hierarchical regression analyses: Step 1 demographics, Step 2 baseline affect, Step 3 weekend experiences predicting Time 2 and Time 3 affect.
Interpret coefficients, significance levels, and variance explained to evaluate hypotheses.
method.models: Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA); hierarchical multiple regression
method.outcome_variable: Affective states (hostility, sadness, fear, fatigue, joviality, self-assurance, serenity)
method.independent_variables: Weekend recovery experiences: relaxation, mastery, control, psychological detachment; non-work hassles
method.control_variables: Baseline affective states (Time 1) and demographic variables

results.summary: Hierarchical regressions showed that relaxation, mastery, and psychological detachment during the weekend increased positive affective states (joviality, serenity, self-assurance) and, in the case of relaxation, decreased negative affective states such as fear and hostility. Non-work hassles heightened negative affect (hostility, fear, sadness, fatigue). Effects were strongest at the end of the weekend (Time 2) and attenuated but remained significant for some outcomes during the workweek (Time 3). Control was generally not a significant predictor once other experiences were included.
